edges.description: Impaired pacemaker function or conduction slows atrial activation and can produce pauses, bradycardia, and low-output symptoms.
Reduced pacemaker current slows sinoatrial node firing and can produce sinus bradycardia, pauses, and hypoperfusion symptoms.
edges.source: HCN4 Pacemaker Current Loss of Function

edges.description: Some HCN4 familial bradycardia pedigrees include left ventricular noncompaction as part of the segregating phenotype.
edges.source: HCN4 Pacemaker Current Loss of Function

edges.description: Sodium-current loss of function can impair impulse initiation or propagation, leading to bradyarrhythmia and symptomatic pauses.
edges.source: SCN5A Sodium Current Loss of Function

edges.description: Reduced sinoatrial node firing manifests as bradycardia.
Sinoatrial node dysfunction can produce sinus pauses or exit block.
edges.target: Sinus Pauses or Sinoatrial Exit Block

edges.description: Impaired rate adaptation causes chronotropic incompetence during physiologic demand.
Sinus node dysfunction increases susceptibility to atrial fibrillation.
Rhythm instability from sinus node dysfunction can produce palpitations.
Severe pauses or slow rates cause presyncope and syncope.
Severe bradycardia, sinus pauses, or asystole reduce cerebral perfusion, producing syncope and the risk of asystolic sudden death.

nodes.description: A supraventricular tachyarrhythmia with disorganized atrial electrical activity and an irregularly irregular ventricular response. In familial sick sinus syndrome, sinoatrial node dysfunction and atrial remodeling increase susceptibility to atrial fibrillation, which frequently occurs as the tachycardic component of bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome.

evidence.snippet: SAN dysfunction (SND) can cause complex cardiac arrhythmias that can manifest as severe sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest, chronotropic incompetence and increased susceptibility to atrial fibrillation, among other cardiac conditions.

nodes.description: Reduced sinoatrial node firing or conduction produces sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest, sinoatrial exit block, chronotropic incompetence, and alternating bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome; symptoms arise when pauses or slow rates reduce cerebral or systemic perfusion.

evidence.snippet: SAN dysfunction (SND) can cause complex cardiac arrhythmias that can manifest as severe sinus bradycardia, sinus arrest, chronotropic incompetence and increased susceptibility to atrial fibrillation, among other cardiac conditions.
Clinical symptoms result from the hypoperfusion of end organs.

nodes.description: Abnormally slow heart rate arising from impaired impulse generation in the sinoatrial node. In familial sick sinus syndrome, inherited pacemaker or ion-channel defects slow diastolic depolarization and sinoatrial node firing, producing sinus bradycardia that may be persistent or intermittent.

evidence.snippet: Electrocardiography findings include sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses or arrest, sinoatrial exit block, chronotropic incompetence, or alternating bradycardia and tachycardia (i.e., bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome).

nodes.description: Inability of the heart rate to increase appropriately to meet metabolic demand during exertion, typically defined as failure to reach a target fraction of the age-predicted maximum heart rate. In familial sick sinus syndrome, impaired sinoatrial node rate adaptation limits exercise capacity and manifests as fatigue and exertional intolerance.

evidence.snippet: The patient's inability to reach a heart rate of at least 80% of their predicted maximum (220 beats per minute - age) may indicate chronotropic incompetence, which is present in 50% of patients with sinus node dysfunction.

nodes.description: A pervasive sense of tiredness and reduced exercise tolerance. In familial sick sinus syndrome, fatigue arises from low cardiac output during episodes of bradycardia and from the impaired heart-rate response of chronotropic incompetence, which together limit oxygen delivery during physical activity.

evidence.snippet: Other symptoms include palpitations, decreased physical activity tolerance, angina, muscular fatigue, or oliguria.

nodes.description: HCN4 variants impair the hyperpolarization-activated pacemaker current in sinoatrial node cells. Dominant-negative or gating-shift effects reduce pacemaker current availability and slow diastolic depolarization, producing bradycardia and sinus node dysfunction.
nodes.id: HCN4 Pacemaker Current Loss of Function

biological_processes: regulation of monoatomic ion transmembrane transport
cell_types: cardiac pacemaker cell of sinoatrial node

evidence.snippet: One of these, p.Gly482Arg in HCN4, segregated with the combined bradycardia and LVNC phenotype in the entire family.
In electrophysiological studies, all found HCN4 mutations showed a more negative voltage dependence of activation, consistent with the observed bradycardia.
Mutant subunits were nonfunctional and exerted dominant-negative effects on wild-type current.

nodes.description: Germline variation can disturb sinoatrial node pacemaker-cell impulse generation, cardiac conduction, and heart-rate adaptation to physiologic demand.

cell_types: cardiac pacemaker cell of sinoatrial node

evidence.snippet: The sinoatrial node (SAN) is the primary pacemaker of the mammalian heart, initiating its electrical activation and ensuring that the heart's functional cardiac output meets physiological demand.
SND has a complex aetiology, with both pre-existing disease and heritable genetic variation predisposing individuals to this pathology.

nodes.description: A structural cardiomyopathy in which the left ventricular myocardium shows prominent trabeculations and deep intertrabecular recesses with a thin compacted layer, reflecting arrested compaction during myocardial development. In familial sick sinus syndrome it co-segregates with the bradycardia phenotype in HCN4-related pedigrees, marking a developmental cardiac overlap of the disorder.

evidence.snippet: Subsequent screening of HCN4 in 3 additional families with the same clinical combination of bradycardia and LVNC identified HCN4 mutations in each.

nodes.description: Subjective awareness of an irregular, rapid, or forceful heartbeat. In familial sick sinus syndrome, palpitations commonly reflect the rhythm instability of bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome, in which periods of sinoatrial node failure alternate with atrial tachyarrhythmias such as atrial fibrillation.

evidence.snippet: Other symptoms include palpitations, decreased physical activity tolerance, angina, muscular fatigue, or oliguria.

nodes.description: SCN5A variants reduce Nav1.5 sodium current through gating, trafficking, or temperature-sensitive loss-of-function mechanisms, lowering myocardial excitability and impairing cardiac conduction in familial or congenital sick sinus syndrome.
nodes.id: SCN5A Sodium Current Loss of Function

biological_processes: cardiac muscle cell action potential involved in contraction
cell_types: cardiac pacemaker cell of sinoatrial node

evidence.snippet: Probands from three kindreds exhibited compound heterozygosity for six distinct SCN5A alleles, including two mutations previously associated with dominant disorders of cardiac excitability.

Biophysical characterization of the mutants using heterologously expressed recombinant human heart sodium channels demonstrate loss of function or significant impairments in channel gating (inactivation) that predict reduced myocardial excitability.
At body temperature, Nav1.5-G1712S additionally exhibited an enhanced slow inactivation and an impaired recovery from inactivation.

nodes.description: Intermittent failure of sinoatrial node impulse formation (sinus arrest or pause) or of conduction of the impulse out of the node into the atrium (sinoatrial exit block), producing transient asystolic gaps on the electrocardiogram. In familial sick sinus syndrome these pauses reflect the underlying inherited pacemaker and conduction defect and can precipitate presyncope or syncope.
nodes.id: Sinus Pauses or Sinoatrial Exit Block

evidence.snippet: Electrocardiography findings include sinus bradycardia, sinus pauses or arrest, sinoatrial exit block, chronotropic incompetence, or alternating bradycardia and tachycardia (i.e., bradycardia-tachycardia syndrome).

nodes.description: Loss of effective cardiac output from severe bradyarrhythmia, prolonged sinus pauses, or asystole causes transient cerebral hypoperfusion (syncope and presyncope) and, when a pause does not terminate, carries a risk of asystolic sudden cardiac death. In familial sick sinus syndrome these bradyarrhythmic events are the shared clinical endpoint, and in pedigrees with cardiomyopathy overlap (for example LMNA disease) sudden death is part of the familial phenotype.

evidence.snippet: About 50% of patients present with cerebral hypoperfusion (e.g., syncope, presyncope, lightheadedness, cerebrovascular accident).
The proband is a 63-year-old male with a family history of individuals (>10) with sinus node dysfunction, ventricular arrhythmia, cardiomyopathy, heart failure, and sudden death.

nodes.description: Transient loss of consciousness (syncope) or near-loss of consciousness with lightheadedness (presyncope) caused by abrupt cerebral hypoperfusion. In familial sick sinus syndrome these episodes result from severe bradycardia, prolonged sinus pauses, or asystole that transiently reduce cardiac output and cerebral blood flow.

evidence.snippet: About 50% of patients present with cerebral hypoperfusion (e.g., syncope, presyncope, lightheadedness, cerebrovascular accident).
